items.quote: Only 2 buyer-side contact role(s) on this opp, below the multi-thread bar.

metrics.narrative: Pep manages 150-200 suppliers (13 Feb 2026 scenario session) and described a manual onboarding cycle ranging from 7 days to 9 months due to manual document chasing (Christina Behm, discovery call 17 Dec 2025). The team also cited a rising volume of untracked contract exceptions and carve-outs over the last five years. No dollar-value ROI or cost-of-inaction has ever been quantified by the buyer, so the business case is directional, not numeric.

sources: All four read calls, 21 Nov 2025 - 13 Feb 2026
champion.narrative: Christina Behm has been the most consistent point of contact across all four read calls (21 Nov 2025, 17 Dec 2025, 16 Jan 2026, 13 Feb 2026) and every logged Next Step entry since, driving scheduling, requesting pricing, and gathering internal use cases. However, she has not demonstrated confirmed access to budget authority or an economic buyer, and her language shifted from active coordination to a vaguer 'working through internal approvals' holding pattern starting 26 May 2026, with a promised end-of-month update (per her 9 Jun 2026 message) that has not arrived as of 9 Jul 2026. This reads as a developing champion who may be losing internal traction, not a confirmed one.

competition.narrative: No competing external vendor has ever been named in any call, field, or Next Step entry; Competitors__c is blank in Salesforce (a hygiene gap, not a knowledge gap). The one consistently named alternative is Pep building the capability internally on its existing Pep Connect platform, stated directly by Tim Drost on 17 Dec 2025 ('we looked at building this, and it still could happen') and never formally ruled out since. The real competitive threat is a build-vs-buy decision, not a vendor bake-off.

identify_pain.narrative: Across the 21 Nov 2025 intro call and 17 Dec 2025 discovery call, Christina Behm and Jillian Lushman described a fully manual supplier onboarding and document process (COIs, W-9s, accounting docs) with no systematic way to track contract exceptions and liability carve-outs ('the knowledge of those exceptions live in Jillian's head and sometimes mine too,' Christina Behm, 17 Dec 2025), and no integrated view of supplier risk or performance. Tim Drost added that contract governance (expiring MSAs, COIs, SOWs, evolving redline terms) is 'archaic' and a stated priority to fix (17 Dec 2025).

paper_process.narrative: No MSA, order form, or contracting discussion has occurred at any point. The furthest the deal has progressed commercially is an initial pricing estimate and literature shared on 18 Feb 2026; no redlines, procurement steps, or signature path have been raised in any call or Next Step entry.

economic_buyer.narrative: No Contact Role, call, or Salesforce field names a CFO, CPO, or budget owner for this deal. The most senior person to ever join a call, Tim Drost (VP Sourcing Operations), engaged on the 17 Dec 2025 discovery call and confirmed the effort's seriousness ('we are engaging a number of folks and gonna make a decision here on a path forward') but has not appeared on or clearly responded to a call or email since roughly 27 Mar to 13 Apr 2026 (about 3 months before this sweep). Christina Behm carries the Decision Maker Contact Role in Salesforce, but on 17 Dec 2025 she stated the project had no budget information and no timeline, which is inconsistent with confirmed economic-buyer authority.

sources: Next Step entries, 26 May 2026 and 9 Jun 2026
decision_process.narrative: Christina Behm said on 26 May 2026 the team was 'working through internal approvals' but named no specific approver, gate, or committee, and repeated on 9 Jun 2026 that she would provide an update 'shortly' (still not received as of this 9 Jul 2026 sweep, roughly 6 weeks later). Tim Drost said on 17 Dec 2025 that Pep was evaluating multiple vendors as well as the option to build in-house, but the actual decision mechanics between those paths have never been described.

decision_criteria.narrative: Module scope was refined live across calls: the 16 Jan 2026 demo narrowed interest to iSupplier, iRisk (lite), and iContract, and the 13 Feb 2026 scenario session confirmed that selection with preliminary user and admin counts sent to shape the quote. Specific functional asks also surfaced in that session: conditional/branching questionnaire logic, task delegation/re-routing when an approver is out of office, portal white-labeling, and third-party risk-provider integrations (Ecovadis, D&B). No formal weighted scorecard has been shared by the buyer.

items.name: Close date pushed 4 weeks (Jun 29 → Jul 28)
items.summary: Close date moved a full month, the second push in under two weeks, suggesting the deal is losing momentum rather than hitting a one-off snag; no accompanying note explains the cause.

day_summary.overall: The only action on the 15th was another close-date push, this time by a full month (June 29 to July 28). Coming right on top of a similar slip two weeks earlier (June 16 to June 29), this is the second consecutive delay and points to a deal that's stalling rather than just slipping a few days — likely stuck on internal buyer process rather than a minor scheduling hiccup. No emails, calls, or meetings logged, so there's no visible explanation for the delay in the record.
